learning_outcomes.description: Understand the basic terminology of ethical theory
Understand the central debates in ethics (breadth and depth)
Demonstrate effective communication and inquiry skills in the context of group discussion on issues in ethics
Conduct independent philosophical research and inquiry
Identify, analyse and evaluate arguments or points of view
Understand the role of ethics in everyday life, including some characterisations of the philosophical questions: Why by moral? What is a good life?
Demonstrate a capacity for creative thought, effective communication and high quality of written expression in the context of research and essay writing

assessments.task_description:
Tutorials will include activities that record and assess participation. These will take the form of submitting questions and comments based on a stimulus, answering reflective prompts, participation in class debates and/or other collaborative group activities. Additional details about allocation of points towards the participation total will be made available via the Blackboard Page and/or by your tutors at tutorials.

Limited Use of AI permitted

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.


Criteria

Your tutorial participation will be assessed according to the following criteria: 

1.             Evidence of participation in discussions.

2.             Evidence of preparation for the tutorial, most notably through completing the set readings.

3.             Quality of contributions to discussions.

4.             Fostering of group discussions, through courteous and respectful interactions with staff and students.

5.             Attendance is necessary to fulfil the above criteria, as per the course requirements, but marks cannot be awarded for attendance alone.

 

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission instructions will vary according to the participation activity.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Task

Students will be provided with a choice of topics via the Assessment Instructions Section of the Blackboard page. Students will need to address the essay question by constructing an argument for a particular answer to one question. Length is 1500 words (+/- 10%).


Referencing and bibliography

  • Include all cited works in a bibliography. Bibliography and referencing are not included in the essay word count.
  • There is no standard referencing style for this course. Check the referencing style guides: https://web.library.uq.edu.au/research-tools-techniques/referencing/referencing-style-guides. You are permitted any recognized referencing style you wish, so long as it includes page numbers where possible (i.e. where the source itself includes page numbers, and where the reference is for specific points attributable to other authors, page numbers must be included).


Use of AI Permitted

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.


Criteria

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Task

The exam is 90 minutes plus 10 minutes of planning. The exam will include both short-answer and long-form writing tasks to test all of the skills we have been developing through semester.


Use of AI Prohibited

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.


Criteria

Students will be assessed on:


1.        Ability to recognize key issues in the question.

2.        Ability to structure an argument.

3.        Use of evidence.

4.        Engagement with academic scholarship and/or debates.

5.        Clear and concise written expression, well presented.


Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to ethics

(1) Course Introduction, and (2) Introduction to ethics: The Hot Hours and the Cool Hours; the ubiquity of values;, some key relevant empirical areas for moral theory; Relationship between law, religion, and ethics. Divine Command theory and the Euthyphro problem. 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction to ethics (1) Course Introduction, and (2) Introduction to ethics: The Hot Hours and the Cool Hours; the ubiquity of values;, some key relevant empirical areas for moral theory; Relationship between law, religion, and ethics. Divine Command theory and the Euthyphro problem.

learning_activities.topic:
Scepticism about ethics

Disagreement about what matters; ‘evolutionary debunking arguments’; scepticism and relativism.   

Tutorials begin in Week 2. To prepare for your tutorial, check the tutorial set reading/instructions on Blackboard and Talis Reading List. 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Scepticism about ethics Disagreement about what matters; ‘evolutionary debunking arguments’; scepticism and relativism. Tutorials begin in Week 2. To prepare for your tutorial, check the tutorial set reading/instructions on Blackboard and Talis Reading List.

learning_activities.topic:
Foundations

Ethics and wellbeing; ethics and rationality.  

Royal Queensland Show Holiday: Tutorials are cancelled this week. 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Foundations Ethics and wellbeing; ethics and rationality. Royal Queensland Show Holiday: Tutorials are cancelled this week.

learning_activities.topic:
Kantian Ethics

Deontology as an objectivist position; The Nature of Reasons; A Good Will; Categorical Imperative and universalizability; Being Treated as Means and Ends; The Kingdom of Ends.

To prepare for your tutorial, check the tutorial set reading/instructions on Blackboard and Talis Reading List.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Kantian Ethics Deontology as an objectivist position; The Nature of Reasons; A Good Will; Categorical Imperative and universalizability; Being Treated as Means and Ends; The Kingdom of Ends. To prepare for your tutorial, check the tutorial set reading/instructions on Blackboard and Talis Reading List.

learning_activities.topic:
Kantian Ethics: Critique and Application

Criticisms of Kant's Theory; the nature of the self and free will presumed in Kant's theory; Deontology in application: Lying, Duties to the Poor, Human Rights, Voluntary Assisted Dying; Punishment.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Kantian Ethics: Critique and Application Criticisms of Kant's Theory; the nature of the self and free will presumed in Kant's theory; Deontology in application: Lying, Duties to the Poor, Human Rights, Voluntary Assisted Dying; Punishment.

learning_activities.topic:
Utilitarianism

Consequentialism; Types of Consequentialism and Utilitarianism; The Value Theory, Aggregative Maximisation; Equality.  


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Utilitarianism Consequentialism; Types of Consequentialism and Utilitarianism; The Value Theory, Aggregative Maximisation; Equality.

learning_activities.topic:
Utilitarianism: Critique and Application

Criticisms of Utilitarianism and Replies; The Self Presupposed in Utilitarianism; Utilitarianism in application: Lying, Duties to the Poor, Human Rights, Voluntary Assisted Dying, Punishment.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Utilitarianism: Critique and Application Criticisms of Utilitarianism and Replies; The Self Presupposed in Utilitarianism; Utilitarianism in application: Lying, Duties to the Poor, Human Rights, Voluntary Assisted Dying, Punishment.

learning_activities.topic:
Aristotle and Virtue ethics

Teleology; Human function and purpose; The nature of virtues and human flourishing; Doctrine of the mean; Rationality of the passions; Distinctive features of virtue ethics as opposed to reductionistic ethics. 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Aristotle and Virtue ethics Teleology; Human function and purpose; The nature of virtues and human flourishing; Doctrine of the mean; Rationality of the passions; Distinctive features of virtue ethics as opposed to reductionistic ethics.

learning_activities.topic:
Virtue Ethics: Critique and Application

Criticisms of Virtue Ethics; Virtue Ethics in Application: Honesty, Compassion, Generosity, Virtues in Christianity and Buddhism, Professional Virtues. 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Virtue Ethics: Critique and Application Criticisms of Virtue Ethics; Virtue Ethics in Application: Honesty, Compassion, Generosity, Virtues in Christianity and Buddhism, Professional Virtues.

learning_activities.topic:
Feminist Approaches

Introduction to the variety of feminist thought; Feminine and Feminist Ethics, Ethics of Care, Epistemological Questions in Feminism.



Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05, L06, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: Feminist Approaches Introduction to the variety of feminist thought; Feminine and Feminist Ethics, Ethics of Care, Epistemological Questions in Feminism.

learning_activities.topic:
Indigenous Approaches

Key features in indigenous world views; relational and place-based approaches; understandings of the self in indigenous thinking; custodial ethic.



Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Indigenous Approaches Key features in indigenous world views; relational and place-based approaches; understandings of the self in indigenous thinking; custodial ethic.

learning_activities.topic:
Non-Human Animals and the Environment

Moral standing of animals and nature; how environmental values function.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Non-Human Animals and the Environment Moral standing of animals and nature; how environmental values function.
